content:
# John Chapter 13 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 13 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 13:1 — "he loved them to the end" vs. "he loved them to the very end" and structural divergence

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having loved the own who [were] in the world, to [the] end he loved them" | Preserves Greek word order; "to the end" for eis telos; "the own" for tous idious |
| LOGOS | "having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end" | Standard English order; "his own" for tous idious |
| PNEUMA | "He had loved his disciples during his ministry on earth, and now he loved them to the very end." | "Disciples" replaces "his own"; "during his ministry on earth" interprets "in the world"; adds "very" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "His own" preserves the theological weight of tous idious (Jesus' own people). "To the end" maintains the deliberate ambiguity of eis telos (temporal and qualitative).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "disciples" narrows "his own" and adds interpretive expansions. EMET's word order is awkward.

---

## 13:2 — "During supper" vs. "It was time for supper" and "put it into the heart of Judas" vs. "prompted Judas"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And supper happening, the devil already having put into the heart of Ioudas [son] of Simon Iskariotes" | Literal genitive absolute; "supper happening" for deipnou ginomenou |
| LOGOS | "During supper, when the devil had already put it into the heart of Judas Iscariot, Simon's son" | Natural temporal clause |
| PNEUMA | "It was time for supper, and the devil had already prompted Judas, son of Simon Iscariot, to betray Jesus" | "Prompted" simplifies "put into the heart"; "it was time for" shifts meaning |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Put it into the heart" preserves the Greek beblekatos eis ten kardian and the sinister intimacy of the devil's action.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "prompted" is too neutral — the Greek describes something planted in the heart. EMET's genitive absolute is too literal for English.

---

## 13:3 — "all things into his hands" vs. "authority over everything"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Father has given all things into the hands of him, and that from God he came forth and to the God he goes" | Literal; present tense "has given"; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going back to God" | Pluperfect for narrative sequence |
| PNEUMA | "the Father had given him authority over everything and that he had come from God and would return to God" | "Authority over everything" interprets "all things into his hands" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "All things into his hands" is the concrete image the Greek provides — "authority over everything" is an abstraction that loses the embodied metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's abstraction loses the physical imagery that is crucial in a chapter about hands and feet.

---

## 13:4 — "laid aside his outer garments" vs. "took off his robe" and "tied it around his waist" vs. "girded himself"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he rises from the supper and lays down the garments, and having taken a towel, he girded himself" | Historical present; "girded" for diazonnumi |
| LOGOS | "rose from supper. He laid aside his outer garments, and taking a towel, tied it around his waist" | Past tense; "outer garments" specifies; "tied around waist" for diezosen |
| PNEUMA | "he got up from the table, took off his robe, wrapped a towel around his waist" | "Robe" (singular) for himatia (plural garments); "wrapped" for girded |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Laid aside his outer garments" (plural) is accurate — himatia is plural. "Tied it around his waist" is clear and faithful.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's singular "robe" misses the plural. EMET's "girded" is archaic.

---

## 13:7 — "you will know after these things" vs. "afterward you will understand" vs. "someday you will"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What I do you do not know now, but you will know after these things" | Literal meta tauta; ginosko = "know" |
| LOGOS | "What I am doing you do not understand now, but afterward you will understand" | "Understand" for ginosko; "afterward" for meta tauta |
| PNEUMA | "You don't understand now what I am doing, but someday you will" | "Someday" is vaguer than "afterward"; omits the verb in the second clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Afterward" is precise — meta tauta points to a specific later time (post-resurrection understanding), not a vague "someday."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "someday" is too indefinite.

---

## 13:8 — "you have no share with me" vs. "you won't belong to me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you have no part with me" | Literal meros; "part" = portion/share |
| LOGOS | "you have no share with me" | "Share" — same concept, slightly more natural |
| PNEUMA | "you won't belong to me" | "Belong" shifts from participation/inheritance to ownership |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "share." The Greek meros (share/portion) implies participation in Christ's inheritance, not mere belonging.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "belong" loses the inheritance/participation connotation.

---

## 13:10 — "completely clean" vs. "clean wholly" vs. "entirely clean"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "is clean wholly" | Literal holos katharos; inverted for emphasis |
| LOGOS | "is completely clean" | Natural English |
| PNEUMA | "to be entirely clean" | Same meaning, different adverb; restructures clause |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "completely clean." Minor stylistic choice — all three capture the meaning.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 13:16 — "Truly, truly" vs. "Amen, amen" vs. "I tell you the solemn truth" and "servant" vs. "slave"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Amen, amen, I say to you, a slave is not greater than the lord of him" | Transliterates amen; doulos = "slave" (literal) |
| LOGOS | "Truly, truly, I say to you, a servant is not greater than his master" | "Servant" for doulos; "master" for kurios |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the solemn truth: slaves are not greater than their master" | Collapses double amen; plural "slaves"; "master" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Servant" for doulos is the established translation convention in this context, though "slave" (EMET) is technically more precise. The doubled "Truly, truly" must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The doulos/servant vs. slave question is noted — EMET's "slave" is more historically accurate, but "servant" is adopted for readability. PNEUMA's collapse of the double amen is not accepted.

---

## 13:18 — "lifted his heel against me" vs. "turned against me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] eating the bread of me lifted up against me the heel of him" | Literal Psalm 41:9 citation preserving Hebrew idiom |
| LOGOS | "He who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me" | Preserves the idiom in natural English |
| PNEUMA | "The one who eats my food has turned against me" | "Turned against" replaces the Hebrew idiom; "food" for "bread" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Lifted his heel against me" is the biblical idiom from Psalm 41:9 and should be preserved in a direct Scripture quotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "turned against" loses the vivid OT image. This is a quotation and should be rendered faithfully.

---

## 13:19 — "that I am he" vs. "that I am" vs. "that I AM"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that I am" | Literal ego eimi without predicate — preserves the divine name echo |
| LOGOS | "that I am he" | Adds "he" for English grammar while retaining ego eimi |
| PNEUMA | "that I AM" | Capitalizes to signal the divine name allusion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "I am he" — the added pronoun makes the clause grammatical in English while the context preserves the ego eimi resonance. PNEUMA's capitalization is an interpretive choice that would be better as a footnote.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's bare "I am" is grammatically incomplete in English. PNEUMA's ALL CAPS is a visual interpretation not in the manuscript.

---

## 13:23 — "reclining at the bosom of Jesus" vs. "reclining at table close to Jesus" vs. "sitting next to Jesus"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "was reclining at the bosom of the Yeshua" | Literal en tO kolpO; preserves the 1:18 echo |
| LOGOS | "was reclining at table close to Jesus" | "Close to" — interprets kolpos as proximity; adds "at table" |
| PNEUMA | "was sitting next to Jesus at the table" | "Sitting" replaces "reclining"; "next to" for en tO kolpO |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with a note that the Greek kolpos ("bosom") echoes 1:18 where the Son is "at the bosom" of the Father. "Close to" captures the physical sense while the theological echo is noted in commentary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's "at the bosom" preserves the important intertextual link but sounds archaic. PNEUMA's "sitting" is anachronistic for a Roman-era reclining banquet.

---

## 13:25 — "leaned back upon the breast of Jesus" vs. "leaning back against Jesus" vs. "leaned over to Jesus"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "having leaned back thus upon the breast of the Yeshua" | Literal epi to stethos; "breast" for stethos |
| LOGOS | "leaning back against Jesus" | Simplifies the physical description |
| PNEUMA | "leaned over to Jesus" | Further simplifies — loses the physical intimacy entirely |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Leaning back against Jesus" preserves the physical closeness without the archaic "breast."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "leaned over" suggests a different posture entirely.

---

## 13:27 — "What you are going to do, do quickly" vs. "Hurry and do what you're going to do"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What you do, do quickly" | Literal; present tense "what you do" for ho poieis |
| LOGOS | "What you are going to do, do quickly" | "Going to do" — futural sense |
| PNEUMA | "Hurry and do what you're going to do" | "Hurry" adds urgency; restructures the command |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The structure "What you are going to do, do quickly" preserves the stark, commanding tone. PNEUMA's "Hurry" adds a word not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 13:30 — "And it was night" vs. "going out into the night"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that [one] went out immediately. And it was night." | Preserves the separate clause; "that one" for ekeinos |
| LOGOS | "he immediately went out. And it was night." | Same structure, natural pronouns |
| PNEUMA | "So Judas left at once, going out into the night." | Names Judas; merges the two clauses; "into the night" makes night a destination |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "And it was night" must stand as its own sentence — its power comes from its stark isolation. PNEUMA's merger ("going out into the night") makes darkness a destination rather than a cosmic statement.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The three-word sentence "And it was night" is one of John's most powerful literary moments and must not be diluted.

---

## 13:31 — "Now is the Son of Man glorified" vs. "The time has come for the Son of Man to enter into his glory"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Now the Son of the Man has been glorified, and the God has been glorified in him" | Aorist passive edoxasthe; articles preserved |
| LOGOS | "Now is the Son of Man glorified, and God is glorified in him" | Present passive for dramatic immediacy |
| PNEUMA | "The time has come for the Son of Man to enter into his glory, and God will be glorified because of him" | Future tense "will be glorified"; "enter into his glory" for edoxasthe; "because of" for en autO |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek aorist edoxasthe functions here as a dramatic aorist — the glorification is so certain it is spoken of as already accomplished. "Now is... glorified" captures this rhetorical force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's future tense and expansion lose the dramatic immediacy of the aorist.

---

## 13:33 — "Little children" vs. "Dear children"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Little children" | Literal teknia |
| LOGOS | "Little children" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Dear children" | "Dear" softens the diminutive; less literal |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Little children" (EMET/LOGOS). Teknia is a diminutive and "little" preserves that affectionate quality.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 13:34 — "just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another" vs. "Just as I have loved you, you should love each other"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "just as I loved you, that you also love one another" | Aorist egapesa; hina clause |
| LOGOS | "just as I have loved you, you also are to love one another" | Present perfect "have loved"; obligation "are to" |
| PNEUMA | "Just as I have loved you, you should love each other" | "Should" for obligation; "each other" for allelous |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Are to love" conveys the imperative force of the hina clause more strongly than PNEUMA's "should."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence; all three capture the essential meaning.

---

## 13:35 — "By this all people will know that you are my disciples" vs. "Your love for one another will prove to the world that you are my disciples"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "By this all will know that you are disciples of me, if you have love among one another" | Literal en toutO; "among" for en |
| LOGOS | "By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another" | Adds "people" to clarify pantes; "for" |
| PNEUMA | "Your love for one another will prove to the world that you are my disciples" | Restructures entirely; "prove to the world" replaces "by this all will know"; drops conditional "if" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The conditional structure ("if you have love") is important — the sign is contingent on actual practice, not guaranteed.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA drops the conditional "if," which changes the rhetorical force.

---

## 13:37 — "I will lay down my life for you" vs. "I'm ready to die for you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The life of me I will lay down for you" | Literal; emphatic word order |
| LOGOS | "I will lay down my life for you" | Natural English; echoes Good Shepherd language (10:11) |
| PNEUMA | "I'm ready to die for you" | Dynamic equivalent; loses the "lay down my life" phrase that links to 10:11 |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Lay down my life" (ten psychen mou thesO) deliberately echoes Jesus' own words in 10:11, 15, 17-18. This intertextual link must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's "ready to die" loses the verbal echo with the Good Shepherd discourse.

---

## 13:38 — "the rooster will not crow till you have denied me three times" vs. "before the rooster crows tomorrow morning, you will deny three times that you even know me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[the] rooster will by no means crow until you deny me three times" | Literal ou me + subjunctive (emphatic negation) |
| LOGOS | "the rooster will not crow till you have denied me three times" | Standard English; "till" for heos |
| PNEUMA | "before the rooster crows tomorrow morning, you will deny three times that you even know me" | Adds "tomorrow morning"; adds "that you even know me" — interpretive expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Greek says nothing about "tomorrow morning" or "that you even know me" — these are interpretive additions.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA adds dramatic detail not in the source text.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adding words/phrases not in Greek | 7 | "very" (v.1), "during his ministry on earth" (v.1), "tomorrow morning" (v.38), "that you even know me" (v.38), "dear" (v.33), "to the world" (v.35) |
| PNEUMA restructuring sentences to lose key literary features | 3 | "And it was night" merged (v.30), conditional "if" dropped (v.35), "lay down my life" paraphrased (v.37) |
| EMET preserving historical present tense | 4 | vv.4, 6, 24, 31 — "rises," "comes," "says," "nods" |
| EMET preserving Greek word order awkwardly | 4 | vv.1, 18, 25, 37 — inverted possessives and clauses |
| Key theological terms diverging | 3 | meros: share/part/belong (v.8), kolpos: bosom/close/next to (v.23), ego eimi: I am/I am he/I AM (v.19) |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA near-identical | ~30 | Most verses show SOPHIA adopting LOGOS verbatim |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

John 13 reveals the same tripartite pattern seen in chapter 12: EMET provides a hyper-literal rendering that preserves Greek syntax, historical present tenses, and Hebrew name forms; LOGOS provides the formal-equivalent translation that SOPHIA almost always adopts; PNEUMA produces a readable dynamic translation that frequently adds interpretive content and restructures sentences for modern accessibility.

The most theologically significant divergences in this chapter center on: (1) the kolpos/bosom language in v.23, where EMET preserves the crucial echo of 1:18 that the other translators smooth over; (2) the ego eimi in v.19, where each translator handles the divine-name allusion differently; (3) the "lay down my life" phrase in v.37, where PNEUMA loses the intertextual link to the Good Shepherd discourse; and (4) "And it was night" in v.30, where PNEUMA's restructuring dilutes one of John's most powerful literary moments.

A notable finding: EMET's literal approach occasionally preserves intertextual connections (kolpos in v.23, "lay down my life" in v.37) that even LOGOS smooths over. The arbiter's consensus benefits from EMET's textual sensitivity even when the final rendering follows LOGOS.

---
*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA — 2026-03-05*
